subtopics.name: French Society During the Late Eighteenth Century

questions.question: Two Treatises of Government was written by:
questions.explanation: Two Treatises of Government was written by John Locke. In this, he has sought to refute the doctrine of the divine and absolute right monarch.

questions.question: Passive citizens of France were:
questions.explanation: Only men above 25 years of age who paid taxes equal to at least 3 days of a labourer’s wage were given the status of active citizens, that is, they were entitled to vote. The remaining men and all women were classed as passive citizens.

questions.question: Marseilles is a:
questions.explanation: Marseilles, composed by the poet Roget de L’Isle. It was sung for the first time by volunteers from Marseilles as they marched into Paris and so got its name. The Marseillaise is now the national anthem of France.

questions.question: Directory means:

options.option: An executive made up of five members

questions.explanation: A new constitution was introduced which denied the vote to non-propertied sections of society. It provided for two elected legislative councils. These then appointed a Directory, an executive made up of five members.

questions.question: Napoleon Bonaparte was defeated in:
questions.explanation: Napoleon Bonaparte was finally defeated at Waterloo in 1815.

questions.question: Why Bastille was hated by people?

options.option: It stood for the despotic power of the military.
It stood for the despotic power of the clergy.
It stood for the despotic power of the king.
It stood for the despotic power of the citizens.

questions.explanation: Bastille was hated by all, because it stood for the despotic power of the king. The fortress was demolished and its stone fragments were sold in the markets to all those who wished to keep a souvenir of its destruction.

questions.question: The storming of the Bastille occurred on the morning of?
questions.explanation: On the morning of 14 July 1789, the city of Paris was in a state of alarm. The king had commanded troops to move into the city. Rumors spread that he would soon order the army to open fire upon the citizens. Some 7,000 men and women gathered in front of the town hall and decided to form a people’s militia. They broke into a number of government buildings in search of arms.

questions.question: What is the Old Regime?

options.option: The society and institutions of France before 1789
The society and institutions of France after 1789
The period when the French society divided into a different estate.
The period when the storming of the Bastille occurred

questions.explanation: The term Old Regime is usually used to describe the society and institutions of France before 1789.

questions.question: Whom did Louis XVI get married?
questions.explanation: In 1774, Louis XVI of the Bourbon family of kings ascended the throne of France. He was 20 years old and married to the Austrian princess Marie Antoinette.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the reason of empty treasure?

options.option: Food supplies to the ordinary citizens.
The cost of maintaining an extravagant court.

questions.explanation: Long years of war had drained the financial resources of France. Added to this was the cost of maintaining an extravagant court at the immense palace of Versailles. To meet its regular expenses, such as the cost of maintaining an army, the court, running government offices or universities, the state was forced to increase taxes.

questions.question: 1st estate comprised of which group?
questions.explanation: 1st estate comprised of Clergy

questions.question: 2nd estate comprised of which group?
questions.explanation: 2nd estate comprised of Nobility

questions.question: Which of the following group comprised the Third Estate comprised?
questions.explanation: The Third Estate comprised poor servants, small peasants, landless labourers, peasants artisans, big businessmen, merchants and lawyers.

questions.question: What was the unit of currency in France, that discontinued in 1794?
questions.explanation: Livre was the unit of currency in France, discontinued in 1794

questions.question: Which group enjoyed certain privileges by birth?
questions.explanation: The members of the first two estates, that is, the clergy and the nobility, enjoyed certain privileges by birth.

questions.question: Which group was exempted from paying feudal dues?
questions.explanation: The nobles enjoyed feudal privileges. These included feudal dues, which they extracted from the peasants.

questions.question: Which of the following work were peasants obliged to perform?

options.option: To work in lord’s house and field

questions.explanation: Peasants were obliged to render services to the lord - to work in his house and fields - to serve in the army or to participate in building roads

questions.question: Which group were obliged to render service to in the army or to participate in building roads?
questions.explanation: Peasants were obliged to render services to the lord - to work in his house and fields - to serve in the army or to participate in building roads

questions.question: Who was one of the most important of the politically active women in revolutionary France?
questions.explanation: Olympe de Gouges was one of the most important of the politically active women in revolutionary France. She protested against the Constitution and the Declaration of Rights of Man and Citizen as they excluded women from basic rights that each human being was entitled to

questions.question: Taille was to be paid directly to?
questions.explanation: Taille was a kind of tax to be paid directly to the state

questions.question: How much amount of tithe was levied from peasants by the church?

options.option: One-tenth of the income of family members

questions.explanation: The Church extracted its share of taxes called tithes from the peasants , comprising one-tenth of the agricultural produce.

questions.question: Which group of persons invested with special functions in the church?
questions.explanation: Clergy were the group of persons invested with special functions in the church.

questions.question: Which of the following was not the reason of subsistence crisis?

options.option: Worker’s wages did not keep pace with the rise in prices
The rapid increase in the demand for food grains

questions.explanation: Occurrence of a great famine was not the reason of subsistence crisis

questions.question: The speech of Robespierre was printed in which newspaper?
questions.explanation: On 7 February 1794, Robespierre made a speech at the Convention, which was then carried by the newspaper Le Moniteur Universel.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a natural and inalienable right in the Constitution of France?
questions.explanation: The Constitution began with a Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen. Rights such as the right to life, freedom of speech, freedom of opinion, equality before law, were established as natural and inalienable rights, that is, they belonged to each human being by birth and could not be taken away.

questions.question: The duty to protect citizen’s natural rights was vested to whom?
questions.explanation: It was the duty of the state to protect each citizen’s natural rights.

questions.question: Who got the right to vote in the France Constitution of 1791?
questions.explanation: Not all citizens had the right to vote. Only men above 25 years of age who paid taxes equal to at least 3 days of a labourer’s wage were given the status of active citizens, that is, they were entitled to vote.

questions.question: Strength lies in unity is portrayed by which political symbols?

options.option: The bundle of rods or fasces

questions.explanation: The bundle of rods or fasces: One rod can be easily broken, but not an entire bundle. Strength lies in unity.

questions.question: The status of active citizens was given to whom?

options.option: Only the man above 25 years paying taxes equal to at least 3 days of a labourer’s wage
Only a man belonging to the higher bracket of taxpayer
Only the man who had the property
Only the members of National Assembly

questions.explanation: Only men above 25 years of age who paid taxes equal to at least 3 days of a labourer’s wage were given the status of active citizens.

questions.question: The political instability of the Directory paved the way for the rise of which military dictator?
questions.explanation: The political instability of the Directory paved the way for the rise of a military dictator, Napoleon Bonaparte.

questions.question: The most revolutionary social reforms of the Jacobin regime was?

options.option: The abolition of slavery in the French colonies

questions.explanation: One of the most revolutionary social reforms of the Jacobin regime was the abolition of slavery in the French colonies.

questions.question: The description relating to the division of power within the government between the legislative, the executive and the judiciary is mentioned in which book?
questions.explanation: In The Spirit of the Laws, Montesquieu proposed a division of power within the government between the legislative, the executive and the judiciary.

questions.question: Who suggested the division of power within the government between the legislative, the executive and the judiciary?
questions.explanation: Montesquieu

questions.question: While drafting the constitution of 1791, what was the main objective of the National Assembly in France?

options.option: To give right of freedom to women
To give equal right to vote

questions.explanation: The main objective of National Assembly in France was to limit the powers of Monarch.

questions.question: Name an influential pamphlet written by Abbe Sieyes.
questions.explanation: Abbe Sieyes, originally a priest, wrote an influential pamphlet called What is the Third Estate?

questions.question: Castle or stately residence belonging to a king or a nobleman is called as?
questions.explanation: Chateau - Castle or stately residence belonging to a king or a nobleman.

questions.question: Which of the following law was not made by Robespierre?

options.option: Peasants were to take their grains to the cities to sell.
Laws were made to fix the maximum wages and prices.
The prices were fixed by the Monarch.

questions.explanation: The prices were fixed by the government.

questions.question: Who proposed a form of government based on a social contract between people and their representatives?
questions.explanation: Jean Jacques Rousseau proposed a form of government based on a social contract between people and their representatives in his book Social Contract.

questions.question: Which book John Locke criticized the doctrine of the divine and absolute right of the monarch?
questions.explanation: In the book named Two Treatises of Government, John Locke criticized the doctrine of the divine and absolute right of the monarch.

questions.question: The execution of Louis XVI and Marie Antoinette was done at which place?
questions.explanation: On 21 January, 1793 Louis XVI and his queen Marie Antoinette were executed publicly at the Place de la Concorde.

questions.question: What is being signified by the political symbol The winged woman?
questions.explanation: The winged woman signifies Personification of the law

questions.question: The eye within a triangle radiating light stands for?
questions.explanation: The eye within a triangle radiating light: The all-seeing eye stands for knowledge.

questions.question: Snake biting its tail to form a ring is a symbol of?
questions.explanation: Snake biting its tail to form a ring: Symbol of Eternity.

questions.question: In India, who were inspired by the ideals for which the French fought?

options.option: Tipu Sultan and Ram Mohan Roy
B. R. Ambedkar and Bal Gangadhar Tilak

questions.explanation: In India, Tipu Sultan and Ram Mohan Roy were inspired by the ideals for which the French fought.

questions.question: What was Sceptre?
questions.explanation: Sceptre was the symbol of royal power

questions.question: Give one word for: An extreme situation where the basic means of livelihood are endangered.
questions.explanation: Subsistence crisis is an extreme situation wheret basic means of livelihood are endangered.

questions.question: Who participated in revolts against increasing taxes and food scarcity?
questions.explanation: In the past, peasants and workers had participated in revolts against increasing taxes and food scarcity. But they lacked the means and programmes to carry out full-scale measures that would bring about a change in the social and economic order.

questions.question: Which ports owed their economic prosperity to the flourishing slave trade?
questions.explanation: Port cities like Bordeaux and Nantes owed their economic prosperity to the flourishing slave trade.

questions.question: Reign of Terror ended with the end of which person?
questions.explanation: Robespierre was convicted by a court in July 1794, arrested and on the next day sent to the guillotine.

questions.question: What was Directory?
questions.explanation: Two elected legislative councils would appoint an executive committee of five members, called Directory which would finally run the government.

questions.question: Which of the following is incorrect regarding the subsistence crisis?

options.option: Production of grains could not keep pace with the growing demand.
Things became worse due to a severe winter which reduced the harvest.
The poverty of France rose dramatically in 1789.

questions.explanation: The poverty of France rose dramatically in 1789. [Explanation: The population of France rose dramatically in 1789.]

questions.question: ________ crisis is an extreme situation where the ________ means of livelihood are endangered.
questions.explanation: Subsistence, basic

questions.question: Identify the cause of empty treasury at the time of Louis XVI.

options.option: Rapid increase in the demand for foodgrains

questions.explanation: Cost of maintaining an extravagant court

questions.question: Read the given figure relating to the course of subsistence crises. Fill in the blank boxes with appropriate terms.

options.option: A - Rising incomes, B - Increased number of army
A - Rising food price, B - Increased number of deaths
A - Rising unemployment, B - Increased number of wars

questions.explanation: A - Rising food price, B - Increased number of deaths

questions.question: The tithe was a tax levied by the ________ on the peasants, comprising one-tenth of the ________.
questions.explanation: church, agricultural produce

questions.question: What services were the peasants obliged to render to the lord?

options.option: to work in his house and field

questions.explanation: All of these

questions.question:
Complete the following table:

1. First estate A. Clergy
2. Second estate B. (?)
questions.explanation: Nobility

questions.question: Study the picture and choose the correct option.
questions.explanation: Merchants and court officials

questions.question: A period from ________ became so infamous that it began to be referred to as the ________.
questions.explanation: 1793 to 1794, Reign of Terror

questions.question: Which of the following is not the measures taken by Robespierre to bring equality in French society?

options.option: Citizens were required to eat equality bread, white flour was forbidden.
Use of Sir and Madam for men and women citizen started instead of Citoyen and Citoyenne.
Churches were shut down and their buildings were converted into offices and barracks.
Foods, such as meat and bread were rationed.

questions.explanation: Use of Sir and Madam for men and women citizen started instead of Citoyen and Citoyenne. [Explanation: Use of Citoyen and Citoyenne for men and women citizen started instead of the traditional Sir (Monsieur) and Madam (Madame).]

questions.question: Against whom the Robespierre followed the policy of severe control and punishment?
questions.explanation: all of these

questions.question: Which social group emerged in France in the 18th century?
questions.explanation: Middle class

questions.question: Study the picture the identify the incorrect option:

options.option: This device was used to behead a guilty person
It was a device consisting of one pole and two blades
This is Guillotine, a device, named after inventor Dr. Guillotin

questions.explanation: It was a device consisting of one poles and two blade. [Explanation: It was a device consisting of two poles and a blade.]

questions.question: Refuting the doctrine of divine and absolute right of the monarch was the main idea of ________ which was made public in his ________.

options.option: Jean Jacques Rousseau, The Spirit of the Laws

questions.explanation: John Locke, Two Treatises of Government

questions.question:
Match the following:

Column A Column B
A. Two Treatises of Government 1. Legislative, the executive and the judiciary
B. Social contract 2. Form of government between people and their representatives
C. The Spirit of the Laws 3. Doctrine of the divine and absolute right of Monarch
On 14th July, 1789 the people of the ________ estate attacked the Bastille prison and freed all the prisoners signalling the start of the ________.
questions.explanation: third, revolution

questions.question: Which of the following statement is incorrect?

options.option: The Bastille stood for the democratic power of the king.
On the morning of 14 July 1789, the people of Paris stormed Bastille

questions.explanation: The Bastille stood for the democratic power of the king. [Explanation: The Bastille stood for the despotic power of the king.]

subtopics.name: France Abolishes Monarchy and Becomes a Republic

questions.question: What was the immediate cause of rioting in Paris?
questions.explanation: The high price of bread

questions.question: Two products that were rationed under Robespierre’s government:
questions.explanation: Meat and bread were rationed.

questions.question: Taille was a kind of ________ tax paid to the _______.
questions.explanation: Taille was a tax to be paid directly to the state.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The members of the first two estates were exempted from paying taxes.
Reason (R): They paid a good proportion of their revenue as a tribute to the king.

options.option: A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct
A and R both are correct but R is not the correct explanation of A
A and R both are correct and R is the correct explanation of A

questions.explanation:
A is correct but R is incorrect

[Explanation: The members of the first two estates enjoyed certain privileges by birth.]

questions.question: Pick the correct option:

options.option: People gathered in the Bastille to plan their movement
King had actually ordered the army to open fire upon the citizens
Citizens decided to form a peoples association to deal with the situation
People apprehended King had ordered the army to open fire upon them

questions.explanation: Rumours spread that he would soon order the army to open fire upon the citizens.

questions.question: The event of 14 July 1789 was the beginning of a chain of events. Which ultimately resulted in ________ of the king in France.
questions.explanation: Historians saw it as the beginning of a chain of events that ultimately led to the execution of the king in France.

questions.question: The war added more than a billion livres to a debt that had already risen to more than 2 billion livres. Choose the incorrect statement in the context of livre.

options.option: Issued in coin form - gold, silver, and later copper.

questions.explanation: Livre was the Unit of currency in France that was discontinued in 1794

questions.question: What led to a rapid increase in the demand for foodgrains?
questions.explanation: The population of France rose from about 23 million in 1715 to 28 million in 1789. This led to a rapid increase in the demand for foodgrains.

questions.question: Besides merchants and manufacturers, the third estate included ________ and ________.
questions.explanation: In addition to merchants and manufacturers, the third estate included professions such as lawyers or administrative officials.

questions.question: Such a government should be formed which is based on a social contract between people and their representatives. Who propounded this idea?
questions.explanation: Rousseau proposed a form of government based on a social contract between people and their representatives.

questions.question: ________ was laid on wages and prices. Peasants were forced to sell their grain at a ________ price.
questions.explanation: Robespierre’s government issued laws placing a maximum ceiling on wages and prices. Peasants were forced to transport their grain to the cities and sell it at prices fixed by the government.

questions.question: Women were active participants in the important events of ________ society.
questions.explanation: French

questions.question: Most women of the ________ estate had to work for a living.
questions.explanation: Third

questions.question: Most women did not have access to ________.
questions.explanation: Education

questions.question: Their wages were ________ than those of men.
questions.explanation: Lower

questions.question: The constitution of 1791 vested the Power to make laws in the National Assembly, which was ________ elected.
questions.explanation: Indirectly

questions.question: What did the Constitution begin with?

options.option: Preface to the Rights of Man and Women
Makers' Note on the Bill of Rights
Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen

questions.explanation: Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen.

questions.question: Only ________ above 25 years of age could vote.
questions.explanation: Men

questions.question: The man who paid taxes equal to atleast 3 days of labourers wage were given the status of ________
questions.explanation: Active Citizens

questions.question: The term Old Regime describes the Society and Institutions of France before ________
What was the ratio of the Peasants?
About ________ percent of the land was owned by nobles, the church and other richer members of the third estate.
The feudal dues were extracted from:
questions.explanation: Peasants

chapters.name: HIS Socialism in Europe and the Russian Revolution

questions.question: Louis Blanc in France believed in:

options.option: A government to encourage cooperatives and replace capitalists

questions.explanation: In France Louis Blanc (1813-1882) wanted the government to encourage cooperatives and replace capitalist enterprises.

questions.question: The event, 'Bloody Sunday' was later on called:
questions.explanation: The incident, known as Bloody Sunday, started a series of events that became known as the 1905 Revolution.

questions.question: The Central Powers of World War I were:
questions.explanation: In 1914, war broke out between two European alliances – Germany, Austria, and Turkey (the Central Powers) and France, Britain, and Russia (later Italy and Romania).

questions.question: The Jadidists were those who:
questions.explanation: Jadidists were the Muslim reformers within the Russian empire.

questions.question: A special secret police was formed by Bolsheviks called:
questions.explanation: The secret police (called the Cheka first, and later OGPU and NKVD) punished those who criticised the Bolsheviks.

questions.question: Which group did not believe in Universal Adult Franchise in Russia?
questions.explanation: The liberals did not believe in giving voting rights to women.

questions.question: Which group opposed privileges enjoyed by landowners and wealthy society?
questions.explanation: The conservatives believed in equal distribution of wealth.

questions.question: Which group opened their minds to the need for change?
questions.explanation: The conservatives opposed radicals and the liberals but started accepting some changes after the Russian Revolution.

questions.question: Who was the king of Russia in 1914?
questions.explanation: Tsar Nicholas -II was crowned as the king in 1894. He was abdicated in 1917.

questions.question: Who was the leader of the Bolsheviks?
questions.explanation: Lenin wanted to unite all the people of Russia and so was elected as the leader if the Bolsheviks.

questions.question: Who was the writer of the books The Communist Manifesto and Das Capital?
questions.explanation: Karl Marx was a revolutionary who initiated the ideas of communism and wrote many books on it.

questions.question: What was the other name of the October Revolution?
questions.explanation: Because it was carried by the Bolsheviks who were also called the 'Reds'.

questions.question: What was budeonovka?
questions.explanation: It was the part of new uniform that was designed for the army and officials.

questions.question: When was Comintern formed?
questions.explanation: It was the international union of pro-Bolshevik socialist parties formed in 1919 after the Russian Revolution.

questions.question: Who were kulaks?
questions.explanation: Kulaks were well-to-do farmers of Russia who were forced to do collective farming during the period of Stalin.

questions.question: What was the name of the Russian Parliament?
questions.explanation: Duma was the elected consultative Parliament of Russia.

questions.question: Which calender did Russia use until February 1918?
questions.explanation: The Russians used the Gregorian calender until 1918 after which they started using the Soviet Eternal calender.

questions.question: What slogan was shouted in Russia during the February Revolution?
questions.explanation: They wanted to see all the people as equal so they shouted for food and peace for everyone.

questions.question: What was the Russian Parliament called?
questions.explanation: The Russian elected Parliament was called Duma which made laws for the country.

questions.question: Which of the following refers to women's right to vote?
questions.explanation: Suffragette was a movement to give voting rights to women.

questions.question: Who headed the party after the death of Lenin?
questions.explanation: After Lenin's death Stalin became the head of the Bolshevik party.

questions.question: What does the word Soviet mean?
questions.explanation: Soviet in Russian language means a council or an organisation of workers.

questions.question: Which demands of the following were not included in April Theses of Lenin?
questions.explanation: The demand of formation of Duma was not included in 'April Theses' by Lenin.

questions.question: Name the vessel used in October Revolution.
questions.explanation: Aurora was one of the three vessels used by the Russians during the Russian Revolution.

questions.question: The river in Petrograd was named as?
questions.explanation: Neva is a river that flows in north western part of Russia where Petrograd city is situated.

questions.question: 8th March is celebrated as which day?
questions.explanation: Women's day is celebrated on 8th March to help nations to eliminate discrimination against women.

questions.question: Bolsheviks were also called as?
questions.explanation: Bolsheviks were the ones who fought as revolutionaries in the Russian Revolution.

questions.question: The name of St Petersburg was changed to
questions.explanation: Petersburg sounded too German name so it was changed to Petrograd.

questions.question: What slogan was shouted during the February revolution?
questions.explanation: As there was acute shortage of food and peace in the country.

questions.question: Who was called whites during the Russian Civil war?
questions.explanation: The anti-bolsheviks or pro-tsarists were called as the 'whites'.

questions.question: Bolshevik party was later renamed as?
questions.explanation: In 1918 the bolshevik party changed its name to Russian Communist Party at Lenin's suggestion.

questions.question: Name the Committee organized by Leon Trotskii during February Revolution.
questions.explanation: Trotsky organized this committee and was called the Commissar of the war in the new Soviet government.

questions.question: What was the collective farm called in Russia?
questions.explanation: Kolkhoz is a Russian name for collective farms.

questions.question: Karl Marx was against which society?
questions.explanation: Karl Marx thought that the Serfs exploit the peasants so he was against the accumulation of wealth by few people in the society.

questions.question: Lenin made a peace treaty with which country after World War I?
questions.explanation: After much opposition Lenin made peace treaty with Germany in 1918.

questions.question: Name the Muslim reformers within the Russian empire.
questions.explanation: Jadidist were the Muslim modernist reformers within the Russian empire. They normally called themselves as progressives and intellectuals.

questions.question: How many people practised agriculture in Russia before the Revolution?
questions.explanation: 85% of people did farming in Russia which was very high as compared to other European nations.

questions.question: Who is known as the Father of Communism?
questions.explanation: Karl Marx a German philosopher is regarded as the Father of Communism. He fled from Germany to live in England as he was considered a political threat in Germany.

questions.question: Who nationalised the banks and industries in Russia?
questions.explanation: Lenin nationalised the industries and banks soon after the October Revolution.

questions.question: Under whom the military committee called the Red Guards formed?
questions.explanation: Trotsky formed this committee soon after the October Revolution.

questions.question: What was the secret police of Russia called?
questions.explanation: Cheka was the secret police of Russia which was later called OGPU and NKVD.

questions.question: Who put forward the concept of April theses?
questions.explanation: Lenin put forward the concept of April theses under which banks were nationalised, lands were given to the peasants and war was put to an end.

questions.question: Who sought to build a cooperative community called New Harmony?
questions.explanation: Robert Owen

questions.question: Who in France, wanted the government to encourage cooperatives and replace capitalist enterprises?
questions.explanation: Louis Blanc

questions.question: Who propounded the idea of a communist society?
questions.explanation: Karl Marx

questions.question: Who was the emperor of Russia at the start of the First World War?
questions.explanation: Tsar Nicholas II

questions.question: When did the government suspend the Duma?
questions.explanation: 25th February

questions.question: The Police Headquarters were ransacked on:
questions.explanation: 27th

questions.question: The Tsar was advised by:
questions.explanation: Military Commanders

questions.question: Petrograd had led ________ Revolution.
questions.explanation: February

questions.question: Who was Robert Owen?
questions.explanation: English Manufacturer

questions.question: Here Indiana means?
Louis Blanc wanted the government to encourage cooperatives and replace ________ enterprises.
questions.explanation: Capitalist

questions.question: According to Karl Marx, the profit of Capitalists was produced by ________
questions.explanation: Workers

questions.question: Working hours were ________ and wages were ________.
questions.explanation: Long, poor

questions.question: Unemployment was common, particularly during times of ________ demand for industrial goods.
questions.explanation: Low

questions.question: ________ themselves were often Property Owners and employers.
questions.explanation: Liberals and Radicals

questions.question: Industrialisation brought men, women and children to ________.
questions.explanation: Factories

chapters.name: HIS Nazism and the Rise of Hitler

questions.question: The term Genocidal means:

options.option: A kind of poison that Helmuth feared
Killing on a large-scale leading to the destruction of a large section of people

questions.explanation: Under the shadow of the Second World War, Germany had waged a genocidal war, which resulted in the mass murder of selected groups of innocent civilians of Europe.

questions.question: A war veterans organisation was called:
questions.explanation: The Weimar Republic crushed the uprising with the help of a war veterans organisation called Free Corps.

questions.question: Nationalist Socialist German Workers' Party was renamed as:
questions.explanation: Hitler subsequently took over the organisation and renamed it the National Socialist German Workers’ Party. This party came to be known as the Nazi Party.

questions.question: The Secret State Police was called:
questions.explanation: Apart from the already existing regular police, there was the secret state police called Gestapo.

questions.question: The Nordic German Aryans were in which position in term of racial hierarchy system?
questions.explanation: Aryans of German origin

questions.question: The most infamous film made on Jews was:
questions.explanation: The most infamous film was The Eternal Jew. Orthodox Jews were stereotyped and marked.

questions.question: In which year did Germany surrender to the Allies?
questions.explanation: In May 1945 German surrendered to the allies. The allied power was led by UK France and USSR.

questions.question: Which German minister committed suicide in Berlin in April?
questions.explanation: Joseph Goebbels was the Propaganda Minister in Hitler's Government, who committed suicide alongside Hitler in the month of April. He said that he felt compelled to remain with Hitler "for reasons of humanity and personal loyalty".

questions.question: Which Treaty ended World War 1?
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles was the most important of the peace treaties that brought World War 1 to an end. The Treaty ended the state of war between Germany and the Allied Powers. Germany was demilitarized and had to pay a huge amount of compensation.

questions.question: Who offered Chancellorship to Hitler?
questions.explanation: In 1932 the Nazi party became the largest party with more than 37% votes. So in 1933, President Hindenburg offered the Chancellorship to Hitler, the highest position amongst the cabinet of ministers.

questions.question: When did Hitler try to seize control of Bavaria and capture Berlin?
questions.explanation: Hitler planned to seize control of Bavaria, marched to Berlin and capture power in 1923 but failed.

questions.question: Which party did Hitler join in 1919?
questions.explanation: In 1919 Hitler joined the German Workers Party

questions.question: Who was assigned the responsibility of economic recovery by Hitler?
questions.explanation: Hitler assigned the responsibility of economic recovery to economist Hjalmar Schacht who aimed at full production and full employment through state-funded work creation program.

questions.question: The US entered the 2nd World War due to
questions.explanation: When Japan extended its support to Germany and bombed the US base at Pearl Harbor, US entered the 2nd World War.

questions.question: The Great Depression was a period of
questions.explanation: Wall Street Exchange collapsed in one day, around 13 million shares were sold – the start of economic depression. The national income of the USA halved. Factories shut down, farmers were hit, export fell and speculators took money off the market. The industrial production of Germany reduced to 40%. People lost jobs, paid reduced wages, unemployment at 6 million. Criminal activities increased. Savings of old age lost as current lost its value. Peasants couldn’t fill the stomach. All these factors indicate that the Great Depression was an Economic Crisis.

questions.question: What was the name given to the gas chamber by Nazi?
questions.explanation: Gas chambers were known as disinfection-areas and looked like bathrooms equipped with fake showerheads.

questions.question: Who from the following were not November Criminals?

questions.question: Who were the November Criminals?
questions.explanation: Socialists, Catholics, and Democrats who supported the Weimar Republic after the war became easy targets of attack in the conservative nationalist circles and were called November Criminals.

questions.question: Reichstag refers to
questions.explanation: German parliament is called Reichstag.

questions.question: Hitler's world view was based on the principals of:

questions.question: Which of the following was the State Secret Police?
questions.explanation: The Gestapo were the secret state police and were the strongest and most feared.

questions.question: Hitler's ideas of racialism were based on which of the following thinkers
questions.explanation: Hitler's idea of racialism was borrowed from Charles Darwin-evolution and natural selection. In this idea, Nordic German Aryans were at the top and Jews at the lowest rank.

questions.question: Which of the following was the immediate factor of the Great Depression (1929-1932)?
questions.explanation: In the year 1929, the Wall Street Exchange collapsed. 13 million shares were sold in a single day, which led to great economic depression.

questions.question: When did World War Second end?
questions.explanation: World War Second ended on May 1945.

questions.question: When did Germany invade Poland?
questions.explanation: Germany invaded Poland in September 1939, this started a war with France and England.

questions.question: When was the Tripartite Pact signed?
questions.explanation: The Tripartite Pact was signed between Germany, Italy and Japan in September 1940.

questions.question: Which act established the dictatorship in Germany?
questions.explanation: In the year 1933, the famous Enabling Act was passed which established Dictatorship in Germany.

questions.question: Where was Adolf Hitler born?
questions.explanation: Adolf Hitler was born in Austria.

questions.question: When was Adolf Hitler born?
questions.explanation: Adolf Hitler was born in the year 1889.

questions.question: During the Second World War which country waged a Genocidal War?
questions.explanation: Under the shadow of 2nd world war Germany waged a Genocidal War–mass murder killing 6 million Jews, 200,000 Gypsies, 1million Polish civilians, 70,000 Germans who were considered mentally and physically disabled by gassing them in killing centres like Auschwitz.

questions.question: Who among the following topped the list of undesirables?
questions.explanation: Jews were stereotyped as the killer of Christ and Usurers and hence they were considered as Undesirables.

questions.question: Who were the Desirables?
questions.explanation: According to Nazi ideology, there was no equality among humans but only racial hierarchy. As per Hitler's racism borrowed from Charles Darwin, the Nordic German Aryans were considered pure blood and hence considered as desirables.

questions.question: World War Second began with the German invasion of
questions.explanation: Germany invades Poland in the year 1939, German forces bombard Poland on land and from the air, as Adolf Hitler sought to regain lost territory and ultimately rule Poland. World War II had begun. The German invasion of Poland was a primary reason for the onset of the 2nd World War.

questions.question: In which place was the International War Tribunal setup?
questions.explanation: At the end of World War 2, the international military tribunal was set up at Nuremberg to prosecute Nazi war criminals for crimes against war, crimes against peace and humanity.

questions.question: Which of the following was not a part of Hitler’s policies to exclude Jews?
questions.explanation: Assimilation was not part of Hitler's policy to exclude Jews.

questions.question: Which of the following was not a feature of the new Nazi-style of politics?
questions.explanation: In the new style of nazi politics, there were no ferocious and strong speeches by Hitler.

questions.question: The German Parliament is known as
questions.explanation: During World War 1 the Reichstag - (Diet of the Realm or Imperial Diet) was the Parliament of Germany from 1871 to 1918. Legislation was shared between the Reichstag and the Bundesrat, which was the Imperial Council of the reigning princes of the German States.

questions.question: Which of these countries was related to Allied powers:
questions.explanation: The principal members of the Allies were the United Kingdom, the Soviet Union, the United States, and China (the “Big Four”)

They faced poisonous gas and enemy shelling.

A camp that was surrounded by electrified barbed-wire fences

Trench life was glorified by the media

chapters.name: HIS Forest Society and Colonialism (Internally Assessed)

questions.question: Deforestation means:
questions.explanation: The disappearance of forests is referred to as deforestation.

questions.question: Oak forests in England were used for:
questions.explanation: By the early nineteenth century, oak forests in England were disappearing. This created a problem of timber supply for the Royal Navy as the English ships were built with a regular supply of strong and durable timber.

questions.question: British needed forests in India to:
questions.explanation: The British Forest department required trees which were suitable for building ships and railways.

questions.question: Swidden agriculture in Sri Lanka is called:
questions.explanation: One of the major impacts of European colonialism was on the practice of shifting cultivation or swidden agriculture. It has many local names such as chena in Sri Lanka.

questions.question: A cluster of villages in Bastar was called a:
questions.explanation: In Bastar, Every year there is one big hunt where the headmen of villages in a pargana (cluster of villages) meet and discuss issues of concern, including forests.

questions.question: Choose the appropriate term - The disappearance of forests is referred as ________.
questions.explanation: Deforestation is not a recent problem. The disappearance of forests is referred to as deforestation.

questions.question:
Which of the following is responsible for increase in cultivated area between 1880 and 1920 ?
A. The production of commercial crops like jute, sugar, wheat, etc.
B. In the early 19 th century, the colonial state thought that forests were unproductive.

options.option: A is true but B is false.
A is false but B is true.

questions.explanation: In the colonial period, cultivation expanded rapidly for a variety of reasons. First, the British directly encouraged the production of commercial crops like jute, sugar, wheat and cotton. Second, in the early nineteenth century, the colonial state thought that forests were unproductive. So between 1880 and 1920, cultivated area rose by 6.7 million hectares. Hence Both A and B are true.

questions.question: Which of the following forests was disappearing in England in the early 19th century?
questions.explanation: By the early nineteenth century, oak forests in England were disappearing. This created a problem of timber supply for the Royal Navy.

questions.question: Which of the following industry of England was dependent on oak tree?
questions.explanation: England wanted timber of an oak tree to build ships.

questions.question: Who was the first inspector General of forests in India?
questions.explanation: British decided to invite a German expert, Dietrich Brandis, for advice, and made him the first Inspector General of Forests in India.

questions.question: Which of the following is not true about the plantation industry during the colonial period?

options.option: The colonial government took over the forests and gave vast areas to Indian planters at cheap rates
These areas were enclosed and cleared of forests and planted with tea or coffee
Large areas of natural forests were also cleared to make way for plantations
It was done to meet Europe’s growing need for these commodities

questions.explanation: Large areas of natural forests were also cleared to make way for tea, coffee and rubber plantations to meet Europe’s growing need for these commodities. The colonial government took over the forests and gave vast areas to European planters at cheap rates. These areas were enclosed and cleared of forests, and planted with tea or coffee.

questions.question: Who was Dietrich Brandies?
questions.explanation: British decided to invite a German forest expert, Dietrich Brandis, for advice, and made him the first Inspector General of Forests in India Forest Expert.

questions.question: Where was the imperial Forests Research institute set up?
questions.explanation: The Imperial Forest Research Institute was set up at Dehradun in 1906. The system they taught here was called ‘scientific forestry’.

questions.question: What is Scientific Forestry?

options.option: Conservation of forests with the help of science.
Under this all the forests were divided into three different categories.
Under this natural forests which had lots of different types of trees were cut down and in their place one type of trees were planted.

questions.explanation: In scientific forestry, natural forests which had lots of different types of trees were cut down. In their place, one type of tree was planted in straight rows. This is called a plantation.

questions.question: Which of the following forests act divided the forests into three categories?
questions.explanation: The 1878 Act divided forests into three categories: reserved, protected and village forests.

questions.question: The 1878 Forest Act divided the forests into three categories. Which of the following was not part of that division?
questions.explanation: The 1878 Act divided forests into three categories: reserved, protected, and village forests. There is no Open category.

questions.question: Which of the following were the best forests?
questions.explanation: The 1878 Act divided forests into three categories: reserved, protected and village forests. The best forests were called ‘reserved forests’.

questions.question: Which of the following was not an important factor responsible for deforestation during the colonial era?
questions.explanation: The factors which are responsible for deforestation during the colonial era are the commercialization of agriculture, the plantation industry, expansion of the railway. Local people were not responsible for deforestation during the colonial era.

questions.question: Choose the appropriate term: Under this parts of forests are cut and burnt and the land is cleared for agriculture. The farmers shift to another forest when the land loses fertility.
questions.explanation: In shifting cultivation, parts of the forest are cut and burnt in rotation. Seeds are sown in the ashes after the first monsoon rains, and the crop is harvested by October-November. Such plots are cultivated for a couple of years and then left fallow for 12 to 18 years for the forest to grow back.

questions.question:
European colonial government decided to ban shifting cultivation. Which of the following is/are appropriate reason/s for the same?

  1. They felt that land which was used for cultivation every year could not grow trees for railway timber.
  2. Shifting cultivation made it harder for the government to calculate taxes.
  3. The government wanted to protect the rights of the forest people.
questions.explanation: European foresters regarded this practice as harmful for the forests. They felt that land which was used for cultivation every few years could not grow trees for railway timber. Shifting cultivation also made it harder for the government to calculate taxes. Therefore, the government decided to ban shifting cultivation. As a result, many communities were forcibly displaced from their homes in the forests.

questions.question: Under the colonial government scale of hunting
questions.explanation: Under colonial rule, the scale of hunting increased to such an extent that various species became almost extinct.

questions.question: The Forest Act meant severe hardship for the villagers across the country, because:

options.option: People were forced to steal and if caught, and they had to pay bribes to the forest guards.
Cutting wood, grazing cattle, collecting fruit, roots, hunting and fishing became illegal.
Women who collected fire wood were harassed by guards.

questions.explanation:
The Forest Act meant severe hardship for villagers across the country.
After the Act, all their everyday practices – cutting wood for their houses, grazing their cattle, collecting fruits and roots, hunting and fishing – became illegal.
People were now forced to steal wood from the forests, and if they were caught, they were at the mercy of the forest guards who would take bribes from them.
Women who collected fuelwood were especially worried. It was also common for police constables and forest guards to harass people by demanding free food from them.

questions.question: Where is Bastar Located?
questions.explanation: Bastar is located in the southernmost part of Chhattisgarh.

questions.question:
Which of the following event prompted the people of Bastar to rebell against the colonial government ?
A. The proposal to ban hunting.
B. The proposal to reserve two-thirds of the forests.
questions.explanation: When the colonial government proposed to reserve two-thirds of the forest in 1905, and stop shifting cultivation, hunting and collection of forest produce, the people of Bastar were very worried. And hence event prompted the people of Bastar to rebell against the colonial government.

questions.question: Who was Gunda Dhur?

options.option: He was nationalist who lead the Bastar Forest rebellion.

questions.explanation: Although there was no single leader, many people speak of Gunda Dhur, from village Nethanar, as an important figure in the movement. He was a nationalist who leads the Bastar Forest rebellion.

questions.question: Which of the following method was used by the people of Bastar to invite villagers to rebel against the British?

options.option: They circulated margo boughs, a lump of earth, chilies, and arrows.

questions.explanation: In 1910, mango boughs, a lump of earth, chilies, and arrows, began circulating between villages. These were actually messages inviting villagers to rebel against the British.

questions.question:
What was the result of Bastar Forest Rebellion ?
A. Work on reservation of forests was temporarily suspended.
B. The area to be reserved was reused to roughly half.
C. The colonial government stopped interfering in the management of forests.
questions.explanation: In a major victory for the Bastar rebels, work on reservation was temporarily suspended,and the area to be reserved was reduced to roughly half of that planned before 1910.But the story of the forests and people of Bastar does not end there.After Independence, the same practice of keeping people out of theforests and reserving them for industrial use continued.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most important crop of Java?
questions.explanation: Java is now famous as a rice-producing island in Indonesia.

questions.question: Which of the following colonial power controlled Indonesia?
questions.explanation: Once upon a time, Java was covered mostly with forests. The colonial power in Indonesia was the Dutch.

questions.question: Who were Kalangs?

options.option: They were people of the noble class of Indonesia.
They were a community of skilled forest-cutters and shifting cultivators.

questions.explanation: The Kalangs of Java were a community of skilled forest cutters and shifting cultivators. They were so valuable that in 1755 when the Mataram kingdom of Java split, the 6,000 Kalang families were equally divided between the two kingdoms.

questions.question: He argued that the state had not created the wind, water, earth, and wood, so it could not own it. Who is ‘he’ in the above statement?
questions.explanation: Around 1890, Surontiko Samin of Randublatung village, a teak forest village, began questioning state ownership of the forest. He argued that the state had not created the wind, water, earth, and wood, so it could not own it.

questions.question: With which of the following country the samin forest movement is associated?
questions.explanation: The Samin Movement is an Indonesian nationalist movement founded by Surontiko Samin in north-central Java.

questions.question:
Which of the following method/s was/were used by the Samin’s to revolt against the Dutch?
A. They lay down on their forest land when the Dutch came to survey it.
B. They refused to pay taxes.

options.option: A is false but B is false.
A is true but B is true.

questions.explanation: Some of the Saminists protested by lying down on their land when the Dutch came to survey it, while others refused to pay taxes or fines or perform labour.

questions.question:
To conserve the forests :
A. the people who live near the forests must be involved.
B. The people who live in forests must be displaced.
questions.explanation: Conservation of forests rather than collecting timber has become a more important goal. The government has recognised that in order to meet this goal, the people who live near the forests must be involved.

questions.question: Sacred groves are known as ________.
questions.explanation: Sacred groves are known as sarnas,devarakudu,kan,Rai in many parts of India

questions.question: In Assam men and women were forced to work in which area?
questions.explanation: In Assam both men and women from forest community like the Santhals were recruited to work in tea plantations

questions.question: Which community was famous for forest cutters and sifting agriculture?
questions.explanation: The Kalanga of Java were a community of skilled forest cutters and sifting cultivation.they were so valuable that when the Mataram kingdom of Java split the 6000 family members were split equally between both the kingdoms without them it would have been difficult to harvest teak and build the king's palaces.

questions.question: Kalangs were skilled Forest cutters and ________.
questions.explanation: Shifting Cultivators

questions.question: When did the Mataram Kingdom of Java split?
The Kalang families were equally divided between the ________ Kingdoms.
questions.explanation: Two

questions.question: Who gained control over the Java Forests in the eighteenth century?
questions.explanation: The Dutch

questions.question: The Central Part of Bastar is on a ________.
questions.explanation: Plateau

questions.question: Chhattisgarh plain lies on the ________ of plateau.
questions.explanation: North

questions.question: The people of ________ believe that each village was given its land by the earth.
questions.explanation: Bastar

questions.question: People paid a small fee paid to take some wood from the forests of another village called:
questions.explanation: People paid a small fee called devsari, dand or man in exchange to take some wood from the forests of another village.

questions.question: Who was Brandis by nationality?
questions.explanation: German

questions.question: Who made Brandis the first Inspector General of Forests in India?
questions.explanation: The British

questions.question: Where was the Imperial Forest Research Institute set up in 1906?
questions.explanation: Dehradun

questions.question: When did the Indian Forest Act formed?
Railways created a new demand from:
Railways were important for the movement of ________.
questions.explanation: Imperial troops

questions.question: By 1890 about ________ of track had been laid.
During the early 1850s, in ________ alone 35,000 trees were being cut annually for sleepers.
questions.explanation: Madras Presidency

questions.question: What do you mean by Deforestation?
questions.explanation: Deforestation means disappearance of forests, when trees are cut down and not planted again, it leads to deforestation. It is a man-made disaster. Sometimes deforestation happens when we convert vacant land into farms or cities.  The major cause is when trees are cut down for wants of wood and fuel. It destroys the natural habitat of animals leading to their death.

chapters.name: HIS Pastoralists in the Modern World (PA Only)

questions.question: Gujjar Bakarwals, the pastoral nomads are from:
questions.explanation: Gujjar Bakarwals of Jammu and Kashmir are great herders of goat and sheep.

questions.question: Vast meadows in Garhwal and Kumaon are called:
questions.explanation: Bugyal – Vast meadows in the high mountains. Bugyal is also known as 'Nature's own gardens' in Uttarkhand.

questions.question: What is stubble?

options.option: Lower ends of grain stalks left in the ground after harvesting

questions.explanation: Lower ends of grain stalks left in the ground after harvesting.

questions.question: The Maasai Mara and Samburu National Park in Kenya and Serengeti Park in Tanzania were turned into:
questions.explanation: Large areas of grazing land were also turned into game reserves like the Maasai Mara and Samburu National Park in Kenya and Serengeti Park in Tanzania.

questions.question: Who are nomads?

options.option: The people who do not live at one place but move from one area to another to earn their living.
The people who live along with their animals.
The people who live at one place to earn their living.

questions.explanation: Nomads are people who do not live in one place but move from one area to another to earn their living. In many parts of India, we can see nomadic pastoralists on the move with their herds of goats and sheep, or camels, and cattle.

questions.question:
Which of the following is a pastoral nomadic community of Jammu and Kashmir ?
A. Due to low temperature the high ranges are covered with snow so there are no pastures.
B. The low hills of Shiwaliks provides green pastures top their animals.

options.option: A is true but B is true.
A is false but B is false.

questions.explanation:
A.When the high mountains were covered with snow, the herds were grazed in the low hills.

B.Gujjar moved annually between their summer and winter grazing grounds. In winter, when the high mountains were covered with
snow, they lived with their herds in the low hills of the Siwalik range.

questions.question: Which of the following is a pastoral nomadic community of Himachal Pradesh?
questions.explanation: In a different area of the mountains, the Gaddi shepherds of Himachal Pradesh had a similar cycle of seasonal movement.

questions.question: Which of the following is a pastoral nomadic community of Garhwal and Kumaon?
questions.explanation: To the east, in Garhwal and Kumaon, the Gujjar cattle herders came down to the dry forests of the bhabar in the winter.

questions.question: A dry forested area below the foothills of Garhwal and Kumaon?
questions.explanation: To the east, in Garhwal and Kumaon, the Gujjar cattle herders came down to the dry forests of the bhabar in the winter.

questions.question: Which of the following is a pastoral nomadic community of Maharashtra?
questions.explanation: Dhangars were an important pastoral community of Maharashtra.

questions.question:
Why the Dhangars move towards cental plateau of Maharashtra during the monsoon season ?
A. Due to availability of green pastures.
B. The sheep can not tolerate the wet monsoon conditions prevailing in the Konkan region.

options.option: A is true but B is false.
A is false but B is true.

questions.explanation: With the onset of the monsoon the Dhangars left the Konkan and the coastal areas with their flocks and returned to their settlements on the dry plateau. The sheep could not tolerate the wet monsoon conditions.

questions.question: The autumn crop usually harvested between September and October.
questions.explanation: Kharif – The autumn crop, usually harvested between September and October

questions.question: Which of the following is a pastoral nomadic community of Andhra Pradesh and Karnataka?
questions.explanation: In Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh, the dry central plateau was covered with stone and grass, inhabited by cattle, goat, and sheepherders. The Gollas herded cattle.

questions.question: Which of the following defines the annual movement of Kurumas and Kurubas of Andhra Pradesh and Karnataka?
questions.explanation: In the dry season, they moved to the coastal tracts and left when the rains came.

questions.question: Which of the following is a pastoral nomadic community of Uttar Pradesh, Punjab, Rajasthan, and Madhya Pradesh?
questions.explanation: Banjaras are a well-known group of graziers. They are to be found in the villages of Uttar Pradesh, Punjab, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, and Maharashtra.

questions.question: Which of the following is a pastoral nomadic community of Rajasthan?
questions.explanation: In the deserts of Rajasthan lived the Raikas. The rainfall in the region was meagre and uncertain. On cultivated land, harvests fluctuated every year. Over vast stretches, no crop could be grown. So the Raikas combined cultivation with pastoralism.

questions.question:
Which of the following factors decide the movement of pastoral communities ?
A. They have to judge how long the herds can stay in one area.
B. They have to find where they can find water and pastures.
questions.explanation: The life of these pastoral groups was sustained by a careful consideration of a host of factors. They had to judge how long the herds could stay in one area, and know where they could find water and pasture.

questions.question: Under which of the following law the uncultivated lands were taken over and given to select individuals?
questions.explanation: From the mid-nineteenth century, Waste Land Rules were enacted in various parts of the country. By these Rules, uncultivated lands were taken over and given to select individuals. These individuals were granted various concessions and encouraged to settle these lands.

questions.question: To Which of the following forests, the pastoralist were not given any access?
questions.explanation: Through Forests Acts some forests which produced commercially valuable timber like deodar or sal were declared ‘Reserved’. No pastoralist was allowed.

questions.question: Under which of the following act many pastoral communities were declared as criminal tribes?
questions.explanation: In 1871, the colonial government in India passed the Criminal Tribes Act. By this Act many communities of craftsmen, traders and pastoralists were classified as Criminal Tribes.

questions.question:
Which of the following step/steps was/were taken by the colonial people which adversely affected the lives of the pastoralist?

  1. Waste Land Act was enacted.
  2. Forest Act was passed.
  3. The criminal Tribes Act was enacted.
  4. Taxes were imposed on the grazing of animals.
questions.explanation:
1. From the mid-nineteenth century, Waste Land Rules were enacted in various parts of the country.
  2. By the mid-nineteenth century, various Forest Acts were also being enacted in different provinces.
  3. In 1871, the colonial government in India passed the Criminal Tribes Act. By this Act many communities of craftsmen, traders and pastoralists were classified as Criminal Tribes. 
  4. Pastoralists had to pay tax on every animal they grazed on the pastures.

questions.question:
How the pastoralist cope with the changes brought into their lives due to colonialism?

  1. Some of them reduced the number of cattle.
  2. Other discovered new pastures.
  3. Some of them gave up their nomadic life and started living a settled life.
  4. Some became traders.
questions.explanation: Pastoralists reacted to these changes in a variety of ways. Some reduced the number of cattle in their herds. Others discovered new pastures when a movement to old grazing grounds became difficult. Some migrated to new places. Some became settled peasants cultivating land, others took to more extensive trading.

questions.question:
What was the major problem of the Maasai community during the colonial period?

  1. They lost their grazing land.
  2. They were declared as criminal tribes.
  3. They were confined to an arid zone with uncertain rainfall and poor pastures.
  4. Large areas of grazing land were also turned into game reserves.
questions.explanation: The Maasai lost about 60 percent of their pre-colonial lands. They were confined to an arid zone with uncertain rainfall and poor pastures. Large areas of grazing land were also turned into game reserves like the Maasai Mara and Samburu National Park in Kenya and Serengeti Park in Tanzania.

questions.question: In which of the following country Samburu National Park is located?

questions.question:
What were the effects of colonial rule over the Maasai community?

  1. They lost their grazing land.
  2. The non-availability of grazing grounds led to a steady decline of the animal stock.
  3. Special reserves were created where they were allowed to graze their animals.
  4. Most of them were declared as criminal tribes.
questions.explanation: Large areas of Grazing land were turned into game reserves. Pastoralists were not allowed to enter these reserves; they could neither hunt animals nor graze their herds in these areas. Also, the non-availability of grazing grounds led to a steady decline of the animal stock.

questions.question: Which of the following formed the ruling group of the Maasai Community?
questions.explanation: In pre-colonial times Maasai society was divided into two social categories – elders and warriors. The elders formed the ruling group and met in periodic councils to decide on the affairs of the community and settle disputes.

questions.question: Who were warriors?

options.option: They were the rulers of the Maasai Community.
They were the advisors of the king.

questions.explanation: In pre-colonial times Maasai society was divided into two social categories – elders and warriors. The warriors consisted of younger people, mainly responsible for the protection of the tribe.

questions.question:
Which of the following was/were feature/features of the Maasai community?

  1. The society was divided into warriors and elder.
  2. The elder was the ruling class whereas warriors provided protection to the tribe.
  3. Warriors were responsible for raiding the cattle of other pastoral groups.
  4. All were equally affected due to colonialism.
questions.explanation: In Maasailand, as elsewhere in Africa, not all pastoralists were equally affected by the changes in the colonial period. Hence (iv) is incorrect Only (i), (ii) and (iii) are true.

questions.question:
The traditional authority of both elders and warriors were adversely responsible for the same?

  1. The British imposed various restrictions on raiding and warfare.
  2. They appointed chiefs of different sub-groups.
  3. The created Game Reserves where the whole tribe was allowed to move freely.
questions.explanation: Large areas of grazing land were turned into game reserves. Pastoralists were not allowed to enter these reserves; they could neither hunt animals nor graze their herds in these areas.

The Deccan Plateau lies to the south of the river Narmada. The Western Ghats and the Eastern Ghats mark the western and the eastern edges of the Deccan Plateau respectively.

questions.question: The Western Ghats cause orographic rain. What is orographic rain?
questions.explanation: Orographic rainfall is also known as relief rainfall. It occurs when the rain-bearing moist winds are forced to rise along the western slopes of the Ghats. It results in precipitation on the windward side of the mountains while there is scanty rainfall on the leeward side.

questions.explanation: Western disturbances are extratropical storms that originate in the Mediterranean region and travel eastward toward India, driven by the westerly jet stream. questions.question: The word monsoon is derived from the Arabic word
questions.explanation: The word monsoon is derived from the Arabic word ‘mausim’ which literally means season.

questions.question: The Coriolis force is caused by
questions.explanation: Coriolis force is an apparent force caused by the earth’s rotation. The Coriolis force is responsible for deflecting winds towards the right in the northern hemisphere and towards the left in the southern hemisphere.

questions.question: Tropical cyclones are often very destructive and arrive on the coasts of
questions.explanation: Tropical cyclones are often very destructive. Sometimes, these cyclones arrive at the coasts of Orissa, West Bengal, and Bangladesh. These cyclones cause great damage to life and property.

questions.question: Which of these states has the coldest winter?
questions.explanation: The climate of Mumbai, Chennai, and Kolkata is moderate throughout the year. So, as comparing other three Madhya Pradesh has the coldest winter.

questions.question: What is climate?

options.option: It is cumulation of average rainfall, temperature and humidity of a place.
It refers to the state of the atmosphere over an area at any point of time.
It refers to the sum total of weather conditions and variations over a large area for a long period of time.
It refers to difference between highest and lowest temperature of a place.

questions.explanation: The composite or generally prevailing weather conditions of a region, as temperature, air pressure, humidity, precipitation, sunshine, cloudiness, and winds, throughout the year, averaged over a series of years. Thus it is the sum total of weather conditions and variations over a large area for a long period of time (more than thirty years).

questions.question: Choose the write term: It refers to the state of the atmosphere over an area at any point of time.
questions.explanation: Weather is the state of the atmosphere or meteorological conditions at a particular place and time as regards heat, cloudiness, dryness, sunshine, wind, rain, etc.

questions.question:
Which of the following is/are elements of weather/climate?

  1. Temperature
  2. Wind
  3. Atmospheric pressure
  4. Precipitation
questions.explanation: The elements of weather and climate are the same, i.e. temperature, atmospheric pressure, wind, humidity, and precipitation.

questions.question: Which type of climate prevails in India?
questions.explanation: The climate of India is described as the ‘monsoon’ type.

questions.question: Which of the following State receive most of its rain during October and November?
questions.explanation: Most parts of the country receive rainfall from June to September. But some parts like the Tamil Nadu coast gets a large portion of its rain during October and November.

questions.question:
Which of the following controls the climate of India?

  1. Latitude
  2. Altitude
  3. Air pressure
  4. Distance from the sea
questions.explanation: There are six major controls of the climate of any place. They are latitude, altitude, pressure and wind system, distance from the sea (continentality), ocean currents, and relief features.

questions.question: Through which of the following state the Tropic of Cancer does not pass?
questions.explanation: Tropic of Cancer is the imaginary line at 23.50 degrees North of the Equator. In India Tropic of Cancer passes through 8 States. ( Rajasthan, Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, West Bengal, Tripura, and Mizoram.) Hence it doesn't pass through Manipur.

questions.question: Indian subcontinent experiences comparatively mild winters as compare to Central Asia because of
questions.explanation: The Himalayas prevent the cold winds from Central Asia from entering the subcontinent. It is because of these mountains that this subcontinent experiences comparatively milder winters as compared to central Asia.

questions.question: The Indian landmass is equally divided by the __________.
questions.explanation: The Indian landmass is equally divided by the Tropic of Cancer. Hence, half of India has a tropical climate and another half has a subtropical climate.

questions.question: Which of the following is not part of the seasons of India?
questions.explanation: Four main seasons can be identified in India – the cold weather season, the hot weather season, the advancing monsoon and the retreating monsoon with some regional variations.

questions.question: What is the duration of the cold weather season of India?
questions.explanation: The cold weather season begins from mid-November in northern India and stays till February. December and January are the coldest months in the northern part of India.

questions.question: Which of the following are two coldest months in the northern part of India?
questions.explanation: The cold weather season begins from mid-November in northern India and stays till February. December and January are the coldest months in the northern part of India.

questions.question: Which winds prevail in India during cold weather season?
questions.explanation: During Winters the North-East trade winds prevail over the country. They blow from land to sea and hence, for the most part of the country, it is a dry season.

questions.question: Why most parts of India remains dry during cold weather season?

options.option: The winds blow from land to sea
The winds blow from land to land

questions.explanation: During winter North-East trade winds prevail over the country. They blow from land to sea and hence, for most parts of the country, it is a dry season.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a climatic condition of the cold weather season of India?
questions.explanation: The cold weather is normally marked by clear sky, low temperatures, and low humidity and feeble variable winds.

questions.question: Which of the following crop is benefited because of winter cyclonic disturbances?
questions.explanation: The low-pressure systems, originate over the Mediterranean Sea and western Asia and move into India, along with the westerly flow. They cause the much-needed winter rains over the plains and snowfall in the mountains. Although the total amount of winter rainfall locally known as ‘mahawat’ is small, they are of immense importance for the cultivation of ‘rabi’ crops.

questions.question: What is the duration of the hot weather season in India?
questions.explanation: Due to the apparent northward movement of the sun, the global heat belt shifts northward. As such, from March to May, it is a hot weather season in India.

questions.question: What is loo?

options.option: These are variable winds blowing in cold season.
These are strong, gusty, hot, dry winds blowing during the day over the north and north western India.

questions.explanation: A striking feature of the hot weather season is the ‘loo’. These are strong, gusty, hot, dry winds blowing during the day over the north and northwestern India. Sometimes they even continue until late in the evening. Direct exposure to these winds may even prove to be fatal.

questions.question: Which of the following state is associated with Kaal Baisakhi?
questions.explanation: The hot weather is also the season for localised thunderstorms, associated with violent winds, torrential downpours, often accompanied by hail. In West Bengal, these storms are known as the ‘Kaal Baisakhi’.

questions.question: Which of the following two states are affected by pre-monsoon showers?
questions.explanation: Towards the close of the summer season, pre-monsoon showers are common especially, in Kerala and Karnataka. They help in the early ripening of mangoes, and are often referred to as ‘mango showers’.

questions.question: Which winds prevail in India during the rainy season?
questions.explanation: By early June, the low-pressure condition over the northern plains intensifies. It attracts the trade winds of the southern hemisphere. These south-east trade winds originate over the warm subtropical areas of the southern oceans. They cross the equator and blow in a south-westerly direction entering the Indian peninsula as the south-west monsoon.

questions.question: In which of the following hills Mawsynram located?
questions.explanation: Mawsynram in the southern ranges of the Khasi Hills receives the highest average rainfall in the world.

questions.question: Which of the following form a period of transition from hot rainy seasons to dry winter seasons?
questions.explanation: During October-November, with the apparent movement of the sun towards the south, the monsoon trough or the low-pressure trough over the northern plains becomes weaker. The months of October-November form a period of transition from hot rainy season to dry winter conditions.

questions.question: Which of the following is responsible for the bulk of rainfall in the Coromandal coast ?
questions.explanation: Sometimes, the cyclones arrive at the coasts of Orissa, West Bengal and Bangladesh. The bulk of the rainfall of the Coromandel Coast is derived from depressions and cyclones.

questions.question: Which is the wettest city in India?
questions.explanation: Mawsynram in the southern ranges of the Khasi Hills receives the highest average rainfall in the world. It is the wettest place on earth.

questions.question: Which of the following factor is responsible for breaks in monsoon?
questions.explanation: The phenomenon associated with the monsoon is its tendency to have ‘breaks’ in rainfall. Thus, it has wet and dry spells. In other words, the monsoon rains take place only for a few days at a time. They are interspersed with rainless intervals. These breaks in monsoon are related to the movement of the monsoon trough.

questions.question: ________ refers to the seasonal reversal in the wind direction during a year.
questions.explanation: Monsoon

questions.explanation: Trees in Tropical Rain Forests hold commercial importance. Some of the trees found in this forest are ebony, mahogany, rosewood, rubber and cinchona.

questions.question: The most dominant species of the tropical deciduous forests is
questions.explanation: Teak, Bamboos, sal, shisham, sandalwood, khair, kusum, arjun, mulberry are commercially important species of Tropical Deciduous Forests. Among them, Teak is the most dominant species of this forest.

questions.question: The wet temperate type of forests are found between a height of
